metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the polyglot routing gap: either add `'generic'` to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` in engine_b.py (mapping it to a sentinel that always matches repo extensions), or justify using the SARIF/standalone backend (like gitleaks) which bypasses `_is_applicable()` language routing.
findings.finding: A 'polyglot' opengrep rule for conflict markers requires `languages: [generic]`, but `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` in engine_b.py does not include `'generic'`. `_is_applicable()` returns `(False, 'unsupported_lang')` for any language not in that map, so the conflict-marker detector would be silently skipped (emitting a coverage abstain) instead of running on every file type.
findings.location: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b.py (_LANG_EXTENSIONS / _is_applicable); plan section 'What'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'generic': ('',)` (or a non-empty sentinel like `('.py', '.js', '.ts', '.go', '.rb', '.java', '.rs')` covering all common extensions) to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS`, and note in the plan that the rule must declare `languages: [generic]`. Alternatively, use a SARIF-backend sentinel (like the gitleaks pattern) with a standalone Python script that greps for conflict markers and emits SARIF — this is truly language-agnostic and bypasses the opengrep language-routing constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the mechanism for a language-agnostic opengrep rule in this codebase: either add 'generic' to _LANG_EXTENSIONS (and verify opengrep accepts `languages: [generic]` with --validate), or document that the detector must use a paths-glob applicability guard that matches all source files instead of a languages declaration.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'polyglot (language-agnostic)' opengrep detector, but engine_b._is_applicable skips any detector whose declared language has no entry in _LANG_EXTENSIONS. The word 'generic' — the semgrep/opengrep language token for regex-on-all-files — is absent from _LANG_EXTENSIONS, so a rule declaring `languages: [generic]` will be silently dropped as 'unsupported_lang'. The no-language path (declares no languages AND no paths globs) always applies, but opengrep itself requires a languages field in every rule and will fail --validate without one. Neither route is verified to work for a truly language-agnostic regex rule in this codebase.
findings.location: What section / Scope (builtin detector rule); src/rebar/grounding/engine_b.py _is_applicable (lines 199-236) and _LANG_EXTENSIONS (lines 63-78)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"generic": (".py", ".js", ".ts", ".go", ".rb", ".java", ".rs", ".tf")` (or a broad catch-all tuple) to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` in engine_b.py, and write the detector YAML with `languages: [generic]` — this is the semgrep/opengrep convention for a regex rule that applies to all file types.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that Engine B's scan pipeline can be scoped to diff-added lines only, OR revise the plan to acknowledge that diff-scoping is file-level (not line-level) and update the FP / 'ADDED lines' claims accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the detector flags conflict markers on 'ADDED lines of changed files' and calls this 'Diff-scoped match', but Engine B (engine_b.scan) scans WHOLE files across the entire repo_root. The existing diff-scoping in run_detectors/apply_failclosed (detectors.py:58–75) is file-level only (a match counts if its location file is in changed_files), with no concept of which lines within that file were added. No diff-parsing, patch-reading, or added-line extraction exists anywhere in the engine pipeline. The 'no new engine code' assertion is therefore directly contradicted: to scope to ADDED lines, new diff-ingestion infrastructure would be required.
findings.location: What section: 'flags VCS conflict markers… on ADDED lines of changed files' and 'Diff-scoped match'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the plan to state that the detector fires on changed files (file-level diff-scope, matching the existing run_detectors post-filter) rather than 'ADDED lines'. If true added-line scoping is required, add an explicit scope item for diff-ingestion infrastructure (e.g. reading the unified diff and extracting '+' lines before invoking the engine, or post-filtering engine matches by line number against the diff hunk ranges).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'on ADDED lines' means file-level diff-scoping (what apply_failclosed already provides) or true per-line diff filtering; if the latter, specify the new integration seam required since engine_b.scan has no diff input.
findings.finding: The plan claims the detector flags markers 'on ADDED lines of changed files', but the existing engine_b.scan + apply_failclosed integration operates at file granularity, not line granularity. engine_b.scan scans the whole-repo working tree; apply_failclosed post-filters matches to changed FILES (via `loc in changed`), not to added lines within those files. There is no diff-line filter in the consumer pattern the plan proposes to reuse.
findings.location: What section ('on ADDED lines of changed files'); src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py lines 70-75 (diff-scope logic)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope or AC2 which detector backend/mode will be used for the conflict-marker rule (e.g., opengrep with `languages: [generic]` for plain-text matching, or a different backend), so the 'polyglot' guarantee is grounded in an implementation decision rather than an assumption.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Polyglot regex' and routes through 'engine_b.scan' (the opengrep/SARIF/ast-grep dispatcher), but all existing opengrep builtin detectors declare a specific language list (python, javascript, terraform). Conflict markers require language-agnostic plain-text matching. The plan does not specify which backend mode the new detector rule will use (opengrep `languages: [generic]`, a regex/grep backend, or something else), leaving a real design decision unresolved at the 'builtin detector rule' deliverable.
findings.location: What section: 'builtin detector rule' / Scope: 'A builtin detector rule'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'ADDED lines' scoping requires new diff-hunk parsing logic (not yet in engine_b.scan or run_detectors), or explicitly relax the AC to file-level scoping and update the AC wording accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan names 'ADDED lines of changed files' as the scoping mechanism, but engine_b.scan scans files on disk (the whole file), and run_detectors post-filters to changed_files at file granularity — there is no existing infrastructure to restrict matches to added lines ('+' diff hunks). The new detector will fire on conflict markers anywhere in a changed file, including pre-existing lines, creating potential FPs on files that carry old markers outside the changed hunks.
findings.location: What section / Scope — 'Diff-scoped match; ADDED lines of changed files'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) change the AC to 'conflict markers in changed files' (file-level, matching existing infrastructure) and note the known limitation, or (b) enumerate a new edit target — e.g. a diff-text preprocessor or a post-filter in run_detectors — that extracts added lines before passing them to engine_b.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the chosen engine backend (opengrep/semgrep regex) can reliably match NUL bytes in source files, OR explicitly scope NUL-byte detection out of the initial implementation and add it as a separate follow-up task with its own feasibility note.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)' as a second signal for the same detector, but no NUL-byte detection mechanism exists anywhere in the builtin detector set, engine_b, or the grounding layer. This is a new capability, not a reuse of existing infrastructure, and contradicts the implicit 'no new engine code' framing.
findings.location: What section: 'obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes) on ADDED lines of changed files'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either drop NUL-byte detection from scope (conflict markers are the zero-FP core; NUL bytes require a binary-aware scanner not available via opengrep) or specify the alternative mechanism (e.g. a Python-based SARIF-sentinel that reads raw bytes) that will handle binary/NUL-byte files.
findings.finding: The plan includes 'NUL bytes' (encoding corruption) in scope, but opengrep/semgrep skips files containing NUL bytes entirely (treats them as binary), so a `pattern-regex` rule cannot detect NUL-byte corruption via the opengrep backend.
findings.location: Plan section 'What': 'flags VCS conflict markers … and obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the chosen backend (opengrep/semgrep) can match NUL bytes in text-mode scans and does not skip NUL-containing files as binary before committing to NUL-byte detection in the feature scope.
findings.finding: The plan asserts NUL-byte detection via 'Polyglot regex' but this is an unverified capability claim: opengrep/semgrep treat files containing NUL bytes as binary and typically skip them rather than matching within them. The existing `pattern-regex` usage in the codebase only matches printable characters.
findings.location: What section: 'obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that the DET criterion is present and correctly wired in criteria_routing.json (e.g., 'The DET criterion appears in criteria_routing.json and routes to the deterministic detector').
findings.finding: 'criteria_routing.json entry' is called out as a required deliverable in the Scope section but no acceptance criterion verifies its presence or correctness.
findings.location: Scope section: 'A builtin detector rule + criteria_routing.json entry'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'added lines' means the detector YAML uses a diff-filtered input (e.g., a temporary file of only `+` lines) or whether 'diff-scoped' means file-level only, and update the AC's no-fire guarantee accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan claims to scope matches to 'ADDED lines of changed files', but the existing Engine B + run_detectors infrastructure diff-scopes to changed FILES (not added lines within a hunk). Whole-file scanning means a pre-existing conflict marker in an unchanged part of a file would also fire.
findings.location: What section: 'on ADDED lines of changed files'; Scope section: 'Diff-scoped match'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g., `pytest tests/detectors/test_det_conflict_markers.py -v`) and name the fixture files (e.g., `added_lines_with_markers.diff`, `equals_inside_heredoc.diff`) that exercise the pass/fail cases.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion is paired with a concrete proving command (e.g., a pytest invocation, fixture file, or CLI command); an executing agent cannot determine when each AC is satisfied without inferring an undescribed test harness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all three ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify AC3 as a concrete observable: name the fixture inputs (e.g., a diff with `=======` inside a Python triple-quoted string as an added line) and state the expected output (no finding emitted), so the criterion is evaluable in-session with a deterministic command.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Recall+no-fire eval passes (marker present vs. a legitimate '=======' inside a string/heredoc)') does not specify what the eval mechanism is, what fixture or input represents the false-positive case, or what passing looks like — making it effort-framed rather than outcome-framed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Example rewrite: 'AC3: Running `pytest tests/detectors/test_det_conflict_markers.py::test_no_fire_equals_in_heredoc` against a fixture diff containing `=======` on an added line inside a heredoc produces zero findings; the marker-present fixture produces exactly one advisory finding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering NUL-byte / encoding-corruption detection (e.g., 'A file with a NUL byte on an added line produces an advisory finding; clean text files do not'), or remove NUL-byte detection from the What section scope.
findings.finding: NUL-byte / encoding-corruption detection is described as an in-scope deliverable in the What section but no acceptance criterion covers it — it is an uncovered deliverable (orphan work).
findings.location: What section: 'obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)' vs. Acceptance Criteria (no NUL-byte AC)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the disambiguation rule for '=======' (e.g., must be anchored to line-start AND preceded/followed by a conflict-marker line, or require the full three-marker ensemble) so AC3's no-fire test has a deterministic, verifiable implementation path.
findings.finding: AC3 promises a 'no-fire' result for legitimate '=======' inside a string/heredoc, but the plan never specifies the disambiguation mechanism (e.g., line-start anchoring, branch-name suffix requirement, three-token ensemble). Without this, the implementation path for the false-positive avoidance claim is unobservable from the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Scope section: 'The regex must require all three markers as a set OR anchor '=======' to appear between a <<<<<<< line and a >>>>>>> line within the diff hunk; standalone '=======' lines are not flagged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify the exact regex/heuristic that achieves AC3's no-fire requirement for '=======' in strings/heredocs (e.g., require all three markers to appear in the diff, or require the full 7-character sequences), or relax AC3 to acknowledge that standalone '=======' matches are accepted FPs.
findings.finding: AC3 requires a 'no-fire' result for a legitimate '=======' inside a string/heredoc, but the described approach is a plain polyglot regex on added lines with no disambiguation mechanism — and the plan explicitly drops 'fuzzy' handling. These two commitments are contradictory: a bare '=======' regex will fire on heredoc/string content.
findings.location: AC3 vs. What section: 'Polyglot regex' + 'Explicitly drops the fuzzy dso parts'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether 'on ADDED lines' means (a) file-level scoping only (the existing mechanism — simpler, acceptable for committed markers) or (b) true line-level diff filtering; if (b), spec how the diff hunk line ranges will be intersected with opengrep match line numbers.
findings.finding: The plan claims detection 'on ADDED lines of changed files', but the existing `run_detectors` diff-scoping is file-level (changed_files set membership), not line-level. OpenGrep scans whole files, so any conflict marker present anywhere in a changed file — including pre-existing lines or context lines — would be flagged, not only newly-added (+) lines.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py lines 68-75; plan AC line 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design-doc entry (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or inline design note in `criteria_routing.json` / `detectors.py`) capturing: (a) the DET exec-type contract, (b) the rationale for fail-open/advisory posture, and (c) the policy for what qualifies as a zero-FP deterministic check vs. LLM pass.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new DET criterion class and a new criteria_routing.json entry — a novel pipeline addition — but mentions no update to CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or any design doc capturing the architectural decision (new exec-type, routing contract, and the policy of treating conflict-marker detection as a zero-FP deterministic check rather than an LLM pass).
findings.location: ## Scope / ## What — no documentation artefact mentioned
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/decisions/det-criterion.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section) with: the exec:DET contract, the three canonical marker patterns and NUL-byte rationale, the fail-open/blocking_enabled:false policy, and a note that future reclassifications must meet the zero-FP threshold before using this criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether `detectors.py` requires new wiring code and state it as a definitive in-scope or explicitly out-of-scope decision (remove 'if needed').
findings.finding: The Scope bullet 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py wiring if needed' defers a real design decision — whether detector wiring requires new code in that file — to the executor with an 'if needed' placeholder, which is an ambiguity signal blocking correct scoping.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py wiring if needed'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py wiring if needed' with either 'Add DET pattern registration in src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py' (if required) or explicitly state 'No changes to detectors.py required — routing handled entirely via criteria_routing.json and the existing engine_b consumer pattern'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the new builtin detector YAML file path (e.g. `src/rebar/grounding/detectors/builtin/det_conflict_markers.yaml`) as an explicit named edit target in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope lists 'A builtin detector rule' as a deliverable but does not name the new YAML file path (analogous to src/rebar/grounding/detectors/builtin/security_iac_public_exposure.yaml for the public-exposure DET criterion). This is a concrete edit target that is missing from the enumerated scope.
findings.location: Scope — 'A builtin detector rule + criteria_routing.json entry'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'src/rebar/grounding/detectors/builtin/det_conflict_markers.yaml — new opengrep/regex detector rule with id rebar.builtin.det.conflict-markers (or similar), polyglot, matching <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>  and NUL bytes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the Engine B consumer call is `apply_failclosed` (fail-closed) or a fail-open variant — reconcile the reuse pattern name with the declared `blocking_enabled false` / advisory constraint so both sections agree.
findings.finding: The plan reuses 'engine_b.scan -> apply_failclosed' (What section) but simultaneously declares 'advisory / fail-open (blocking_enabled false)'. The function name 'apply_failclosed' implies fail-closed/blocking semantics, directly contradicting the stated fail-open constraint and AC2 ('no new BLOCK source').
findings.location: What section: 'Reuses the Engine B detector consumer pattern (engine_b.scan -> apply_failclosed)' vs. 'advisory / fail-open (blocking_enabled false)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'apply_failclosed' with the fail-open variant (e.g., apply_failopen or the equivalent pattern) or explicitly note that apply_failclosed is called with blocking_enabled=False to achieve fail-open semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the 'Why' or 'What' section explicitly distinguishing this DET criterion's scope (code-review gate scanning *client* repos' committed diffs) from the existing `check-merge-conflict` pre-commit hook (commit-time gate on the *rebar* repo itself), to pre-empt NIH readings.
findings.finding: The repo already ships a `check-merge-conflict` pre-commit hook (pre-commit-hooks v6.0.0, .pre-commit-config.yaml:19) that runs on every commit locally AND in CI via `pre-commit run --all-files`. The plan's DET criterion flags the same conflict-marker tokens — but the plan never distinguishes that the pre-commit hook guards only the *rebar* repo at commit time, while the code-review gate scans *client* repos' diffs at review time. Without this differentiation stated, the plan reads as NIH-adjacent overlap with an already-committed gate.
findings.location: What section / 'Scope' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Why' section, add: 'Note: rebar's own repo has a check-merge-conflict pre-commit hook, but that guards only rebar contributors. This DET criterion runs in the code-review gate and catches markers in *client* repos' diffs that were already committed (e.g., merged without a pre-commit hook).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the existing `check-merge-conflict` pre-commit hook (already polyglot + gating in CI) and justify what code-review-gate scenario this detector covers that the commit-time hook does not (e.g., PR branches without enforced pre-commit).
findings.finding: The repo already has a `check-merge-conflict` pre-commit hook (pre-commit-hooks v6.0.0) wired into both local commit gates and CI's `pre-commit run --all-files` step. The plan proposes an Engine-B detector for the same signal (VCS conflict markers) without acknowledging this prior art or justifying the duplication.
findings.location: Why / What sections; .pre-commit-config.yaml line 19; .github/workflows/test.yml lines 57-62
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prior art' note to the plan: 'The pre-commit `check-merge-conflict` hook catches this at commit time for local contributors. This detector covers the code-review path for PRs where pre-commit was not enforced (e.g., external forks, direct API pushes).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
